children.name: Dendrobates
children.description: Classic aposematic poison frogs of the Amazon.

children.name: Dendrobates tinctorius
children.description: Brilliant blue/black/yellow; hundreds of colour morphs; Amazonian peoples rubbed parrots against its back to dye feathers — hence dyeing frog.

children.name: Dendrobates auratus
children.description: Extremely variable; introduced to Hawaii; popular in captivity.

children.name: Oophaga
children.description: Egg-feeding frogs with exceptional parental care.

children.name: Oophaga pumilio
children.description: Over 30 distinct colour morphs across Panama; females transport individual tadpoles to bromeliad pools and feed each one unfertilised eggs for weeks.

children.name: Oophaga granulifera
children.description: SW Costa Rica and NW Panama; striking red and green.

children.name: Phyllobates
children.description: The most toxic frogs on Earth.

children.name: Phyllobates terribilis
children.description: The most toxic vertebrate known; one frog carries enough batrachotoxin to kill 10 adult humans; Emberá people apply its skin secretions to blowgun darts — the only frog actually used this way.

children.name: Phyllobates bicolor
children.description: Also used on darts by indigenous peoples; bright orange with black venter.

children.name: Epipedobates
children.description: Phantasmal frogs with medically significant toxins.

children.name: Epipedobates tricolor
children.description: Skin secretion contains epibatidine, 200 times more potent than morphine; led to the discovery of a new class of non-opioid painkiller compounds.

children.name: Ranitomeya
children.description: Tiny thumbnail frogs with monogamous bonds.

children.name: Ranitomeya amazonica
children.description: Classic thumbnail frog; red and black; forms long-term monogamous pair bonds — rare among frogs; male transports tadpoles.
